role_summary: Saint Augustine, Florida native; witness to a Ku Klux Klan parade through the Lincolnville Black neighborhood as a child; one of the African American students who integrated previously all-white St. Augustine public schools in the late 1960s; later educator and non-profit manager; participant in the St. Augustine ACCORD Freedom Trail commemoration project.
ai_reading: Duncan's neighbor counts are moderate (peak 94), with Dr. Robert Bagner Hayling (entry 103) as the top match and the broader cluster including Barbara Edna Vickers (entry 5), Purcell Maurice Conway (entry 99), and the joint Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer interview (entry 4). The Hayling pairing is the catalog's clearest St. Augustine civil-rights-movement cosine: Hayling led the NAACP Youth Council and brought King to St. Augustine in 1964; Vickers organized alongside Hayling as his neighbor; Duncan was a child during that organizing arc and integrated the schools that resulted from it. The catalog flags a +0.89 reading toward Armed Self-Defense on the nonviolence axis; for a 1956-born St. Augustine resident whose civil rights memory is shaped by witnessing KKK violence in childhood, the embedding's pull toward Self-Defense is consistent with a witnessing-to-violence register that places her testimony in continuity with the catalog's documented St. Augustine Hayling-Vickers cohort.
biographical_paragraph: While Duncan's LoC oral history carries a publication-block audit tier (known issues prevent full publication-grade certification, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external sources before citation), the external biographical record is documented through the Civil Rights History Project finding aid for entry 51 [src: 1, src: 2]. Duncan was born in 1956 in Saint Augustine, Florida and grew up in the city's historically Black Lincolnville neighborhood. As a child she witnessed a Ku Klux Klan parade through Lincolnville, the kind of segregationist intimidation event the early-1960s St. Augustine civil rights organizing (led by Dr. Robert Bagner Hayling, catalog entry 103, alongside Barbara Edna Vickers, catalog entry 5) was responding to [src: 2]. She was one of the African American students who integrated previously all-white St. Augustine schools in the late 1960s. She attended St. Johns River College, married Richard Allen Duncan in 1976, and has five children. She worked across her career as an educator and in non-profit management. She participated in the ACCORD Freedom Trail initiative, the St. Augustine civil-rights-movement-commemoration project that documents and marks the sites of the local 1963-1964 organizing campaign [src: 2].
movement_context: Duncan's childhood in 1960s Lincolnville sits inside the most concentrated documented period of civil rights direct action in northeast Florida. Lincolnville, founded by formerly enslaved residents in 1866 immediately south of the colonial-era city core, was both the residential base for St. Augustine's Black population and the organizing infrastructure node out of which the early-1960s campaign ran: Dr. Robert Bagner Hayling's dental practice on Bridge Street and the local NAACP Youth Council meeting spaces sat within walking distance of Duncan's childhood streets [src: 3]. The Klan parade Duncan witnessed as a child belongs to the documentary record of segregationist counter-mobilization that Hayling and his NAACP Youth Council were responding to through 1963 and 1964, including the September 18, 1963 Klan rally at which Hayling, James Hauser, Clyde Jenkins, and James Jackson were captured, beaten, and nearly killed before sheriff's deputies intervened [src: 3, 4]. The school integration cohort Duncan joined in the late 1960s implemented the desegregation order issued in the St. Johns County school district after the Supreme Court's 1968 Green v. County School Board of New Kent County ruling closed the freedom-of-choice loophole that southern districts had used through the mid-1960s to delay compliance with Brown v. Board of Education [src: 5]. The St. Augustine implementation, like the parallel implementations across rural and small-city Florida, placed small numbers of Black students into majority-white school buildings without the structural supports of bused-in cohort size or local civil rights protection, and oral histories from the Florida desegregation generation consistently document the social-isolation and physical-intimidation costs the integrating students absorbed across the late 1960s and early 1970s [src: 5, 6]. Duncan's testimony places her inside that documented cohort, with the additional regional specificity that her schoolhouse desegregation occurred in the same city whose preceding direct-action campaign had drawn Martin Luther King Jr. to its bayfront just four summers earlier [src: 3].
legacy_and_reception: Duncan's later participation in the ACCORD Freedom Trail (the St. Augustine commemoration project founded in 2004 by local civil rights veterans to mark the sites of the 1963-1964 organizing campaign) places her oral history alongside the documentary record the project itself assembles [src: 7]. The ACCORD Civil Rights Museum operates from the Rudcarlie Building on Bridge Street in Lincolnville, within the same residential geography Duncan grew up in, and the Freedom Trail's thirty-one site markers across the city annotate the public locations where the 1964 demonstrations, kneel-ins, and beach wade-ins occurred [src: 7]. Her oral history was recorded for the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) and is held at the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture as joint stewards of the collection [src: 1, src: 2]. The publication-block tier the CRHP audit substrate assigns to her transcript is the conservative gate-keeping designation that constrains direct quotation of the recorded transcript until the Pass 1 through Pass 8 audit issues are resolved; it does not impair the externally-documented record of her witness role or her participation in the school-desegregation cohort [src: 8].

gallery.caption: St. Augustine civil rights demonstrations, 1964, from the Florida Memory project of the State Library and Archives of Florida. The footage documents the spring 1964 SCLC direct-action campaign in St. Augustine, the organizing arc Duncan witnessed as a child in Lincolnville and whose outcome included the school-desegregation order she helped implement four years later.
gallery.photographer: State Library and Archives of Florida (Florida Memory project)

gallery.alt: Lincolnville Historic District historical marker, St. Augustine, Florida
gallery.caption: Historical marker for the Lincolnville Historic District in St. Augustine, the historically Black neighborhood founded in 1866 by formerly enslaved residents where Duncan grew up and witnessed the Ku Klux Klan parade described in her oral history. Lincolnville served as the residential and organizing-infrastructure base for the 1963-1964 St. Augustine civil rights campaign.

gallery.alt: ACCORD Civil Rights Museum in the Rudcarlie Building, Bridge Street, Lincolnville, St. Augustine, Florida
gallery.caption: The ACCORD Civil Rights Museum, housed in the Rudcarlie Building on Bridge Street in the Lincolnville neighborhood. The museum is operated by the ACCORD Freedom Trail commemoration project, which Duncan participated in and which documents and marks the sites of the 1963-1964 St. Augustine civil rights organizing campaign.
